IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—scalable cross-lingual name tagging and linking—by building a pipeline for 282 Wikipedia languages (``The ambitious goal... 282 languages'') using silver-standard annotations, self-training, morphology features from anchor links, and mined translation pairs. Empirical signals reported (morphology features giving 11.1% and 7.1% absolute F gains for Turkish and Uzbek; self-training yielding 6% and 12% gains; and results on non-Wikipedia data comparable to supervised models) indicate the approach materially improves performance and robustness. The large-scale resource release (``All the data sets, resources and systems for 282 languages will be made publicly available as a new benchmark'') makes the work likely to be widely used and to open new research directions in low-resource and multilingual IE. While not necessarily transformative in theory, this is a strong-impact, broadly useful contribution to multilingual NLP.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper largely combines established techniques (BiLSTM-CRF name tagger, NPMI seed selection from Guo et al., iterative self-training, and unsupervised collective entity linking similar to Pan et al. 2015) but applies them at very large scale (282 languages) and integrates several KB-mining steps. Specific potentially novel elements include automatic derivation of language-specific affix lists from Wikipedia anchor markups and the pipeline to mine word translation pairs from cross-lingual links, but most components are non-trivial engineering/integration rather than fundamentally new algorithms. Overall this reads as a moderate, incremental contribution driven by scale and resource-mining rather than a genuinely new method.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses multilingual name tagging and cross-lingual entity linking for 282 languages using NLP/ML methods (BiLSTM-CRF, embeddings, self-training, GIZA++ translation mining, KB-based collective inference) and evaluates on standard resources (DARPA LORELEI, DBpedia) with comparisons to recent baselines (Zhang et al., Tsai et al.). These topics, methods, and evaluations are squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP conferences (multilingual NER/EL community) and are positioned with appropriate related-work framing and empirical ablations (morphology, self-training). Given the methodological and evaluation standards shown in the provided text, the paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP venue.

CLARITY.justification: The manuscript contains a discernible method->experiments structure (sections 2–4) but is poorly organized overall: the high-level motivation and goals (the paragraph beginning 'The ambitious goal of this work...') appear near the end rather than up front, and many paragraphs are duplicated verbatim (e.g., repeated 'Most of the previous work...' blocks), which disrupts flow. Notation and formatting are inconsistent (the NPMI equation and inline footnote markers are corrupted), and figures/tables are referenced but either absent or awkwardly presented (Table 1 is shown but with cramped/misaligned feature descriptions). The writing has frequent grammatical and typesetting issues (LaTeX artifacts, repeated sentences, and encoding glitches in examples), making important parts hard to follow.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper describes a coherent pipeline and reports empirical gains, but there are clear technical problems and missing details. The presented NPMI formula is mathematically incorrect (their Eq. (1) reduces incorrectly to ln p(tag)p(token) rather than the standard PMI or normalized PMI), undermining the seed-selection claim. Key methodological details are underspecified (clustering criterion and n, cross-entropy measure, self-training confidence thresholds, candidate generation/unlinkable criteria, and lack of reported statistical significance), making replication and confidence in the claimed SOTA comparisons weak. The experimental claims (e.g., 5.8%–17.6% lower than supervised, morphology gains, self-training improvements) may be valid but are insufficiently supported in the provided text.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper reports experiments on Wikipedia and Non‑Wikipedia data (Non‑Wikipedia tests in 9 LORELEI languages) and compares to prior work, and presents targeted analyses: morphology feature ablation (Turkish/Uzbek gains of 11.1%/7.1%) and a self‑training learning curve for Turkish (6%/12% gains) which supports some claims. Related work is extensively cited and limitations are partially acknowledged (incomplete Wikipedia anchors; affix‑only morphology). However, evaluation details are incomplete for the claimed 282 languages (results said to be “promising” but not shown), ablations are narrow (few languages/components), and many reproduction details and hyperparameters (τ, cluster counts, confidence thresholds, full linking score formulas) are missing. Overall the experimental coverage is adequate but missing important analyses and full evidence for the broad claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to appropriate recent baselines (e.g., Zhang et al., 2016a; Tsai et al., 2016) and to supervised gold-standard models on the DARPA LORELEI data, reporting F‑scores and stating it outperforms some language-independent methods while trailing gold-supervised models by 5.8–17.6% (Sections 4.2, 4.3). It also measures and analyzes ablations (morphology features, self‑training gains) and cites a wide range of relevant prior work for tagging and linking. Minor details about exact evaluation conditions and all possible recent competing neural baselines are not shown in the excerpt, hence not fully verifiable, so I rate comparisons as good with small omissions.
